_meta.purpose: Warden golden set — HOLDOUT split (rubric §4). VERIFIER-ONLY: never seen during builder tuning, to defeat train-on-test leakage. GATE 3 EVALUATES THIS SPLIT: tier-match ≥90%; HARD rule — any active recall / qualifying settlement → ACT; ONE downward mis-tier on an ACT case = fail. Labels are produced by a fresh-context labeler independent of the runtime tiering logic; ACT cases self-label from real CPSC recalls pulled live from saferproducts.gov on 2026-06-13.

cases.rationale: Active CPSC recall of Vornado portable SRTH tower heaters — miswiring can cause overheating / fire hazard. An active recall on an owned item is ACT by definition (§4 hard rule).
Recent CPSC recall (2025-2026) of Vevor baby swings used for sleep — suffocation hazard; violates the mandatory infant-sleep / inclined-sleeper ban. Active recall → ACT.
Recent CPSC recall (2025) of AliExpress convertible strollers — risk of serious injury or death from a fall hazard; violates the mandatory stroller standard. Active recall → ACT.
Active CPSC recall (2025) of FENGQS electric bikes — lithium-ion fire/burn hazards; risk of serious injury or death. Active recall on an owned item → ACT.

Active CPSC recall of Fisher-Price Rock 'n Play Sleepers (reannounced 2023; at least eight infant deaths). Active recall on an owned item → ACT. Distinct phrasing from the dev case to test generalization, not memorization.
Active CPSC recall after one child died and 70+ incidents (objects/children pulled under the rear). Active recall → ACT.

Should-not-alarm decoy (§5). No matching active recall for plain artificial garland; its only public-record hits are ubiquitous Prop 65 60-day notices (lead/phthalates in decorative PVC) → is_ubiquitous, suppressed to CONTEXT. Must not over-alarm.
Should-not-alarm decoy (§5). No active recall matches; only ubiquitous Prop 65 PVC/phthalate notices → CONTEXT (suppressed noise).

Should-not-alarm decoy (§5) at the baseline edge. Lead-glaze on ceramic dinnerware is a real, widely-known regional/baseline concern (Prop 65 territory). It must land AWARE or CONTEXT — a real but baseline/low-action concern — never an ACT/ADDRESS alarm. Accept AWARE or CONTEXT (both are 'should-not-alarm').

Should-not-alarm decoy (§5). Real ubiquitous Prop 65 60-day notices exist for vinyl floor mats (PVC/DEHP), but no relevant active recall — so the keyword CPSC hits drop and only the ubiquitous Prop 65 noise remains → suppressed to CONTEXT. Must not over-alarm on a sticker that is on everything.

Clearly-clean / novel item — a nonsense string with no real product-noun tokens, so no CPSC recall, no Prop 65 notice, no public action on file. Expect a neutral timestamped record statement (NONE), never a blank screen and never an all-clear (§9). (Note: a 'clean'-sounding item that contains a common noun like 'blanket' will trip real keyword hits — the clean control must be token-clean.)
cases.source_hint: no public record on file → record_statement

cases.rationale: §6 conditioning (higher-exposure context). ZIP 48503 → FLINT, CITY OF (PWSID MI0002310), SDWA violations on EPA ECHO. With the drinks-unfiltered-tap exposure context this surfaces as ADDRESS with a non-null condition.

§6 conditioning (lower-exposure context). With NO ZIP / water context the water hazard is not investigated at all (ZIP-driven source) and is not surfaced. Direction: the higher-exposure context (ADDRESS) must never tier LOWER than this one.
cases.source_hint: no zip context → water hazard not surfaced

cases.rationale: §6 conditioning (higher-exposure context). An infant in the home is the at-risk receptor for an infant-sleeper recall. Active recall → ACT regardless; the at-risk context must NEVER lower the tier relative to the no-context case.
§6 conditioning (lower-exposure context / baseline). Same hazard, no infant context. Active recall → ACT. Direction: the infant-context case must never tier LOWER than this baseline.
